title: Can you social engineer an AI? Plus: AI worms and the nonhuman identity problem
summary: An IBM Security Intelligence podcast examines social engineering attacks against AI systems, including prompt injection exploits against Meta/Instagram, a self-replicating AI worm from the University of Toronto, and findings from the Sophos State of Identity Security 2026 report on nonhuman identity breaches. The episode debates whether AI agents are more susceptible to social engineering than humans and discusses the threat of AI worms and inadequate nonhuman credential management in organizations.
writeup: This IBM Technology podcast episode examines social engineering attacks aimed at AI systems, covering prompt injection exploits against Meta and Instagram, a self-replicating AI worm developed at the University of Toronto, and nonhuman identity breach findings from the Sophos State of Identity Security 2026 report. It matters because AI agents introduce attack surfaces distinct from human targets, and organizations often lack adequate credential management for the nonhuman identities that AI systems rely on. Key takeaways: the discussion debates whether AI agents are more susceptible to social engineering than humans, weighs the threat posed by AI worms, and highlights inadequate nonhuman credential management as a security gap.
